markets.name: Knoxville / Knox County, TN
markets.thesis: TN live-first permit market: KGIS public permits and parcel layers give the fastest verified builder-signal plus skip-trace research loop.

markets.name: Nashville / Davidson County, TN
markets.thesis: High-growth Tennessee perimeter market; use Buildchek/Metro permit evidence before seller promotion.

markets.name: Chattanooga / Hamilton County, TN
markets.thesis: TN builder-sprawl source well; pull permit builders before parcel sourcing.

markets.name: Murfreesboro / Rutherford County, TN
markets.thesis: Hot TN builder-sprawl jurisdiction; CivicPlus permit monitoring should feed buyer validation first.

markets.name: Franklin / Williamson County, TN
markets.thesis: High-value TN infill/perimeter market; direct portal source review before parcel calls.

markets.name: Polk County / Lakeland, FL
markets.thesis: Top inland FL target with builder demand and less coastal insurance friction.

markets.name: Ocala / Marion County, FL
markets.thesis: Inland FL resource well; prioritize permit-active builders and quarter/rural-edge lot criteria.

markets.name: Clermont / Lake County, FL
markets.thesis: Inland Orlando-edge growth market; monitor permit portal migration and builder concentration.

markets.name: Gainesville / Alachua County, FL
markets.thesis: Inland FL permit well with manageable fragmentation.

markets.name: Maricopa County / Phoenix-Mesa, AZ
markets.platform: Maricopa weekly permit reports + Accela cities
markets.thesis: AZ goldmine: weekly permit activity reports normalize Phoenix-metro builder velocity.

markets.name: Tucson / Pima County, AZ
markets.thesis: Secondary AZ permit well; Accela-style builder evidence before seller sourcing.

markets.name: Buckeye, AZ
markets.thesis: Fast-growth Phoenix-edge market; source review and portal monitoring.

markets.name: Raleigh / Wake County, NC
markets.thesis: NC Piedmont corridor source well with direct ArcGIS permit data.

markets.name: Charlotte / Mecklenburg County, NC
markets.platform: Accela + Power BI Daily Building Permits
markets.thesis: Large NC buyer market; use direct permit dashboards and Buildchek coverage.

markets.name: Greensboro / Guilford County, NC
markets.thesis: Piedmont corridor seller/builder well once buyer evidence is captured.

markets.name: Concord / Cabarrus County, NC
markets.thesis: ArcGIS source well for permit and parcel normalization.

markets.name: Austin, TX
markets.thesis: TX open-data beachhead; useful but lower than TN/FL/AZ/NC under current stack.

markets.name: San Antonio, TX
markets.thesis: TX open-data permit well with daily normalization potential.

markets.name: Plano / Collin County, TX
markets.thesis: DFW-edge resource well; direct portal/PermitVector required before seller sourcing.

markets.name: McAllen, TX
markets.thesis: Accela-powered TX resource well; lower priority than open-data TX markets.

sourceCandidates.title: ATC CWPP Travis County BCP Parcels
sourceCandidates.description:
Attribute table fields(alias):

• OBJECTID (Object ID): Unique ID assigned by GIS

• GIS ID (GIS ID): Unique GIS ID used for GIS spatial analysts

• TC_ID (Unique Final Records ID): Unique ID for all Travis County tracts to avoid confusion with multiple names

• TractName (Tract Name): Tract name

• TractLabel (Tract label): Tract label for map making

• OwnerCategory (Name of Land Owner): These are the names of the true landowners, as opposed to the easement holders or land manager.

• EasementHolder (Easement Holder): Easement holders only exist in Conservation Easements, which may be part of the BCP, but may or may not be owned by a managing partner. Some Conservation Easements will be managed by a managing partner, others will be managed independently.

• GISAcres (GIS Acres): Acres calculated by GIS

• SurveyAcres (Survey Acres): Acres calculated by a surveyor from an official final survey, as per closing documents for the tract.

• Perimeter (Perimeter): Parcel Perimeter calculated by GIS, in square meters.

• Macrosite (Macrosite): BCP Macrosite that the parcel is within

• ManagementUnit (Management Unit): BCP Management Unit that the parcel is within

• FiscalYear (Fiscal Year): Fiscal Year of closing, taken from closing documents.

• FundingSource (Funding Source): Funding source for this acquisition (ie: grant, fee simple, etc.)

• AcqMethod (Acquisition Method): Acquisition Method, if fee simple, purchased with grants, transferred as part of a 10a permit (donation)

• ClosingDate (Closing Date): Official closing date

• ParcelType (Parcel Type): Parcel type (BCP or Cave)

• OtherNames (Other Names): A List of other names that the tract has been known by

• DeedDocNum (Deed Document Number): Deed record number from County Clerk

• DeedDocLocation (Deed): Link to T drive location for Deed

• LandManager (Land Manager): Entity that manages the land (ie: Travis County, City of Austin, Private Landowner or contractor name)

• LandManagerContact (Tract Manager): Shows tract managers for all tracts, TC tracts show who is the designated tract manager, this field is to be updated to reflect Fall 2018 TC tract managers

• PublicAccess (Public Access): Shows public access, for display in web-maps.

• DataSource (Data Source): This indicates what data was used to create the parcel boundaries (CAD, TCAD, or Survey). This is intended to give land managers an idea of the accuracy of the boundary layer, as well as to keep track of what parcels have been updated to reflect CAD surveys, etc.

• Address (Address): List of addresses to gates and entry points to the property with the most common entrance listed first.

• Notes (Notes): Notes

• RuleID (RuleID): Layer symbology representation

***

sourceCandidates.title: navigation_control_zones
sourceCandidates.description:
This GIS feature class includes navigation control zones on lake surfaces in the City of Austin, Texas

Navigation Control Zones is part of the Austin City Code, Chapter 8-5 Waterway Access and Use:

§ 8-5-2 NAVIGATION CONTROL ZONES.

(A) Navigation control zones are established on Lake Austin, Town Lake, and Lake Long, including the creeks, sloughs, and coves appurtenant to the lakes. The navigation control zones and the regulations applicable in each zone are as follows:

(1) DANGER indicates a zone within which a person should use caution.

(2) FISHING ONLY indicates a zone in which a person may not use the water for a purpose other than fishing.

(3) KEEP OUT indicates a zone that a person may not enter.

(4) NO PROP BOATS indicates a zone in which a person may not operate a propeller-driven boat.

(5) NO SCUBA indicates a zone in which a person may not use a self-contained underwater breathing apparatus.

(6) NO SKIING indicates a zone in which a person may not use a watercraft to tow a person on water skis or a similar apparatus.

(7) NO SWIMMING indicates a zone in which a person may not enter the water, except in a watercraft or while skiing.

(8) SKIN DIVING ONLY indicates a zone in which a person may not use the water for a purpose other than skin diving.

(9) SKI ONLY indicates a zone in which a person may not enter the water or operate a watercraft except for the purpose of water skiing.

(10) SLOW - NO WAKE indicates a zone in which a person may not operate a watercraft at a speed that creates a foaming or churning wake that curls or breaks.

(B) The boundaries of the navigation control zones are depicted the “Navigation Control Zone Map” on file with the city clerk.

(1) Except as provided in Subsection (2), a navigation control zone boundary is formed by:

(a) a shoreline, dam, pier, or other structure to which it is adjacent;

(b) a perpendicular line from a shoreline, dam, pier, or structure to the nearest navigation buoy that marks the zone; or

(c) a straight line between adjacent navigation buoys that mark the zone.

(2) This paragraph applies to a navigation control zone that is indicated by a single navigation buoy or that has an unmarked boundary. The area immediately around a navigation buoy and from which the buoy can be read during daylight hours is included in the navigation control zone indicated by the buoy.

Source: 1992 Code Section 14-1-3; Ord. 031009-11; Ord. 031211-11.

Ordinance No. 20200206-012

An Ordinance creating slow-no wake navigational control zones on Lake Austin; and creating an offense.

Be it ordained by the City Council of the City of Austin:

Part 1. The council finds that:

(A) Lake Austin is a popular place of recreation for boaters, users of personal watercraft, swimmers, and other members of the public. As a result, Lake Austin is often crowded. This gives rise to the potential for dangerous conflicts and collisions between users.

(B) The area on Lake Austin under and around the Pennybacker Bridge, and the area immediately north of the Tom Miller Dam, near the Oyster Landing Marina and the Walsh Boat Landing, both create a particularly high risk for dangerous collisions on Lake Austin. These areas attract crowds on the lake, there are a number of boat rental vendors, and there are popular boat ramps and marinas.

(C) Restricting boat traffic on Lake Austin by creating SLOW - NO WAKE navigation control zones in these hazardous areas is necessary to protect public safety. Creating these navigation control zones in these areas will protect public safety by calming boat traffic. The navigation control zones will be marked by navigation buoys.

(D) Subsection (B) of City Code§ 8-5-2 (Navigation Control Zones) provides that the boundaries of the navigation control zones are depicted on the "Navigation Control Zone Map" on file with the City Clerk. Exhibit 1 depicts the newly created navigation control zones and will be filed with the City Clerk to be incorporated into the "Navigation Control Zone Map."

PART 2. The council enacts the following:

(A) SLOW - NO WAKE navigation control zones shall be established as shown on the map attached as Exhibit 1. A person operating a watercraft within the areas established as new SLOW - NO WAKE navigation control zones on Exhibit 1 shall comply with subsection (A)(l0) of City Code § 8-5-2 (Navigation Control Zones).

(B) A person commits an offense if the person does not comply with the regulations indicated by the navigation buoys marking the SLOW - NO WAKE navigation control zones.

PART 3. This ordinance takes effect on February 17, 2020.

sourceCandidates.title: MunicipalPlanArea
sourceCandidates.description:
This feature layer includes the planning area boundaries for the City of Buckeye and the City of Surprise, as defined by each City's General Plan [1,2]. This is an aggregated dataset of the Buckeye and Surprise general plan areas.  This aggregation was completed by White Tank Mountains Conservancy (D.Warnecke) on Feb. 6, 2026.  The ESRI Geoprocessing tool ‘Merge’ was used to aggregate the City of Surprise feature "General_Plan_Planning_Area" (last updated July 7, 2025) with the City of Buckeye feature “Buckeye_Planning_Area" (last updated Aug. 7, 2025).  Fields were automatically generated and consolidated from all inputs.  Unused or unnecessary fields were deleted. Two additional fields were added, Name and Name_Expanded, for the purpose of creating labels in map products. The Calculate Geometry function was used to recalculate the fields Sq_Miles and Acres in the merged attribute table. The aggregated feature was named Municipal Plan Areas.

The feature layer, named 'MunicipalPlanArea,' has been crafted to support interactive map displays tailored to the specific needs of WTMC. Adapted from the two original sources, its purpose is to provide a unified view of municipal planning areas within the WTMC Connectivity Initiative planning area. The development of this feature layer involved integrating the MPA Layer from both the City of Surprise, AZ, and the City of Buckeye, AZ, each operating as an independent jurisdiction. This layer is exclusively for internal WTMC use and will not be utilized for further analysis or shared externally.

sourceCandidates.description: 2018-2019 Statewide School Districts for Texas. This information was collected from all 253 central appraisal districts and from the Texas Education Agency. GIS staff of the Texas Legislative Council created the school district boundaries using the 2010 TIGER/Line Shapefile as base geography and made further corrections to match the school district boundary updates and name changes for the 2018-2019 School Year. These changes include lines that are not census geography. Changes to school district boundaries may include one or all of the following types: school district annexations or de-annexations; school district consolidations, deletions or additions; boundary corrections to the Texas Legislative Council database; boundary adjustments due to more spatially accurate data involving land parcels and survey data received from a county central appraisal district. sourceCandidates.title: SAPD SAFFE Zones
sourceCandidates.description:
The central core of SAPD's Community Policing activities is the SAFFE (San Antonio Fear Free Environment) Unit. First established in 1994-95 with 60 officers and supervisors, then enlarged in 1996 with an additional 40 officers, the SAFFE Unit consists of officers who focus on identifying, evaluating and resolving community crime problems with the cooperation and participation of community residents.

SAFFE officers are assigned to specific areas or neighborhoods within the city, and work closely with both residents and the district patrol officers also assigned to those areas. SAFFE officers establish and maintain day-to-day interaction with residents and businesses within their assigned beats, in order to prevent crimes before they happen. SAFFE officers also act as liaisons with other city agencies, work closely with schools and youth programs, coordinate graffiti-removal activities, and serve as resources to residents who wish to take back their neighborhoods from crime and decay.

SAFFE Website

sourceCandidates.title: Structures In Floodplain
sourceCandidates.description:
The points in this layer represent locations of structures In FEMA 100 Year Floodplain, Bexar County, TX. 2023 Study.
The San Antonio Office of Emergency Management conducted a study to determine how many structures are within or intersect the FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area (100 year floodplain) in San Antonio and Bexar County, TX. Locations of structures in floodplain were identified using FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area (flood zones A, AE, AO, AH) (area 35,104 acres), and Texas Google Imagery provided by the Texas Natural Resources Information System (TNRIS). Some of the structures such as miscellaneous residential improvements (e.g., detached open porches, detached garages, detached carports and sheds), electrical transformers, canopies, gas station canopies, gazebos, and small trailers were excluded from the study results. Structures in floodplain were further intersected with Bexar County Appraisal District parcel data and categorized by parcel land use type.

sourceCandidates.title: City of Austin - Urban Rail Pilot Visualization
sourceCandidates.description:
Urban rail is potentially coming to Austin, TX. This project examined the physical and economic development changes that could take place by taking the output of Envision Tomorrow and loading it into the CityEngine Web Viewer.

The project team consisted of Civic Analytics, the City of Austin, and the University of Texas. Using a scenario planning software called Envision Tomorrow, the team added data-heavy building types to city parcels most likely to develop while trying to reach population projections for the year 2030. The output of Envision Tomorrow is a number of helpful analytics and maps that demonstrate impact from various future scenarios. These results were then passed on to local stakeholders for feedback to help a more accurate model.

Taking the output and loading it into CityEngine allowed the team to better visualize the height, form, and density impacts of the new development. Whereas before, development was displayed by showing parcels divided up by color in 2D maps, CityEngine enabled the stakeholders to more readily digest the output and spot the differences between scenarios. Stakeholders were able to spot development that would probably not happen and also ask questions about why some parts of Austin were riper for change than others. Their feedback was crucial as it allowed the project team to refine the analysis and look as accurately as possible into the future.

One of the goals of the project was to examine two scenarios of development: what if we build transit and what if we do not. The CityEngine web viewer enables the average user to swipe between two scenarios and to select a building to learn more about its attributes. The capability of sharing this model via the web cannot be overstated as it allows for increased accessibility and personal freedom to explore the project. 3D models make information easier to digest than maps. This fact makes getting feedback on physical planning projects more efficient, reliable, and easy to do.

sourceCandidates.title: Emerging Projects Austin - Land-use Layer
sourceCandidates.description:
This map includes information about the geographical location, extent, future land use, and status of projects in or around Austin. With a few exceptions, only projects that are more than 10 acres and/or those that will have 20 or more residential units have been included. Some of the exceptions are smaller sites in the urban core that will be more densely developed or larger sites with fewer units that cover large tracts of land. The information is sourced from newspapers, web blogs, web sites, as well as the City's development review process. The information is a snapshot in time and might not depict the actual current status of the project. This information is usually updated on a quarterly basis.
Some of the information presented in the map includes:
  • Proposed Land Use: Projects are categorized by proposed land use on the map.
    • Civic: includes hospitals, government services, educational, religious, meeting and assembly, cemeteries, cultural services
    • Commercial: includes hotels, self storage facilities, commercial recreational spaces, non residential uses that are not solely retail or office
    • Industrial: includes manufacturing, mining, solar farms, warehouses, landfills, warehousing and distribution
    • Miscellaneous:
    • Mixed Use: includes combination of uses, typically residential with non residential uses.
    • Office: office
    • Open Space: includes parks and greenbelts, golf courses, camp grounds, preserves, trails
    • PUD: projects which have been submitted as Planned Unit Developments to the City's development review process.
    • Residential - Duplex : duplexes
    • Residential - Multi Family: includes apartments, attached condominiums, group quarters, retirement housing, three/ fourplexes
    • Residential - Single Family: single family, includes manufactured homes.
    • Retail: retail
    • Transportation: includes rail road facilities, transportation facilities, bus stations, marinas, aviation facilities
    • Utility: electric, waste water, and water utilities
  • Project Status: The database also includes information about the status of the project.Status is categorized as follows -
    • Proposed: Projects that have been proposed for a particular site but are not in the City of Austin's development review process.
    • In Review: Projects that have been submitted to the City of Austin 's review process but have not been approved at the time this database was updated.
    • Approved: Projects which have received approval from the City of Austin's review process, but have not started construction.
    • Active: Projects where construction has started.
    • Outside City of Austin's jurisdiction: Projects which are outside the jurisdiction of the City of Austin and are not tracked for construction updates.
    • Transportation Projects: These could be in or around Austin.
  • Case Number: Case Number for the project in the City of Austin's development review process. Some common Case Type prefixes used in this web map are
    • C14 - Zoning
    • C814 - PUD
    • C12M - MUD
    • C8 - Subdivision
    • C8J - Subdivision Joint City/County
    • SP - Site Plan Administrative Approval
    • SPC - Site Plan Commission Approval
    • for more information regarding case type suffixes and prefixes please refer to this AMANDA Website Manual.
  • More Information: The pop up also contains a link to the City's Development Public Information web page where more information about the project can be found.
For projects in Downtown Austin, please refer to Downtown Austin Emerging Projects.

sourceCandidates.title: Large Trees in Austin, TX
sourceCandidates.description:
Residential development in Austin, Texas is sorely needed as housing prices have risen dramatically over the past few years, pricing many families out of the market. See below for the FED's Austin housing index which more than doubled over the past 10 years.

Building of new housing is challenging for a number of reasons: regulations, economics, engineering, logistics and supply chain. Many regulations limit what can be built on a parcel of land in Austin, and, for this project, I am interested in regulations that limit development based on existing trees.

Often builders need to chop down trees on a property in order to build new houses or apartments. The city generally does not allow trees with diameters larger than 24 inches to be chopped down. It is thus important for a builder to know the size of the trees on a piece of land before they purchase it with the intent of building something new.

sourceCandidates.title: A Connectivity Vision
sourceCandidates.description:
This story is about a very special place, the White Tank Mountains, situated on the urban fringe of one of the fastest-growing metropolitan areas in the western U.S. — Phoenix, Arizona. The story is part one of a four-volume StoryMap collection about the White Tank Mountains Conservancy's (WTMC) Regional Connectivity Initiative. This story serves as the introduction to a broader collection that explores expected urban growth, the ecological reasons behind our connectivity imperative, the diverse natural and cultural resources at stake, and the opportunities for community engagement.

In this story, you will learn about a collaborative planning effort launched in October of 2022 by WTMC to bring together local developers, landowners, land management agencies, the Arizona Game and Fish Department, and the City of Buckeye to explore opportunities to align the needs of wildlife, people, recreation, open space, and flood hazard management.  The culmination of our work to engage these diverse stakeholders is the Conceptual Wildlife Linkage Report [1].  In this story, you will be introduced to this report and a conceptual wildlife linkage, our collective vision for the future. At its core, our mission is to conserve land that sustains wildlife movement between the White Tank Mountains and undeveloped native Sonoran Desert public lands to the west, north, and south. These desert mountains are a highly valued natural asset in the region, with two regional parks and hundreds of thousands of visitors each year. Their beauty and biodiversity enhance recreational opportunities for residents in adjacent cities, as well as tourists from afar. Our connectivity imperative grows as development proceeds within the cities of Buckeye and Surprise, threatening to surround and ecologically isolate the White Tank Mountains ecosystem. Maintaining connectivity would protect the long-term diversity and abundance of plants and animals, and help promote a high quality of life, economic opportunities, and conservation of the rich natural history of this region for generations to come.

In this story, we use maps to introduce the reader to future land use plans within the municipal planning jurisdictions of the cities of Buckeye [2] and Surprise [3], and to take a look at the extent of developed and agricultural lands across Maricopa County, Arizona, as of 2024 [4]. 

Lastly, we present an interactive map for our Conceptual Wildlife Linkage [1] that allows the exploration of individual components of our connectivity plan. Our story collection will continue to evolve as we share updates on the implementation of the Conceptual Wildlife Linkage and add new biological information that deepens our understanding of the species we aim to conserve, guiding our work to establish land conservation priorities to preserve our vision of a regional wildlife linkage.

Citations:

[1] White Tank Mountains Conservancy and Logan Simpson. Conceptual Wildlife Linkage Report: White Tank Mountains Regional Connectivity Initiative, A Regional Plan for Wildlife Connectivity in the West Valley. 2024.

[2] City of Buckeye. imaginebuckeye 2040 General Plan. Amended 2022. Buckeye, 2018. City of Buckeye, General Plan | City of Buckeye. Accessed 13 Dec. 2025.

[3] City of Surprise. Surprise General Plan 2035: Foundation for the Future. Surprise, 2013. City of Surprise, Planning & Zoning | Surprise, AZ - Official Website. Accessed 13 Dec. 2025.

 

[4] Maricopa Association of Governments. Existing Land Use for Maricopa and Pinal Counties, Arizona, 2024. Phoenix: Maricopa Association of Governments. Maricopa Association of Governments Open Data. Accessed 17 Dec. 2025.

sourceCandidates.title: Booming Cities
sourceCandidates.description:
From a regional perspective, the west-valley cities of Peoria, Surprise, Buckeye, and Goodyear are all poised for significant urban growth and expansion. Collectively, these west-valley cities, and others nearby, have a desire to work together on regional strategies to conserve the natural heritage of the western Maricopa County region. These cities are collaborating on ways to align plans for open space, recreation, and conservation of wildlife habitats in a connected and complementary fashion. All agree that a regional approach to achieving shared goals will result in a higher quality environment for residents and wildlife across the region.

In this story, we take a close look at plans for future urban growth within the municipalities of Buckeye and Surprise. Plans are to urbanize the last remaining natural Sonoran Desert lands west, north, and south of the White Tank Mountains. In this story, we look at the future land use, transportation, and open space plans adopted by each City council and presented in their respective General Plans. We also explore Maricopa County flood control and park needs for the region.

This story is the fourth in a four-volume StoryMap collection about the White Tank Mountains Conservancy's Regional Connectivity Initiative (CI).   The focus of this initiative is a very special place, the White Tank Mountains, situated on the urban fringe of one of the fastest-growing metropolitan areas in the western U.S. — Phoenix, Arizona.  This rugged Sonoran Desert mountain ecosystem is a highly valued natural asset in the region, with two regional parks and hundreds of thousands of visitors each year; it is truly an icon that epitomizes the values of the surrounding communities.  In this story collection, you will learn about why WTMC and its many partners care about the urban growth plans for two western Maricopa County cities, Buckeye and Surprise, and the opportunities to build on existing plans for open space, parks, recreation, and flood hazards.  Private and State Trust lands within these two city jurisdictions surround the White Tank Mountains, and all of these lands are subject to development pressures. These future growth plans could fragment and isolate the White Tank Mountains, threatening the long-term biodiversity and sustainability of this desert mountain ecosystem. Preserving connectivity—for both wildlife and people—is essential to countering the ecological impacts of fragmentation and isolation. By working together on thoughtful connectivity solutions, we can shape a future that supports community well-being, economic vitality, and long-term conservation of the region’s rich natural heritage.  

Spatial data used in maps to illustrate future land use, future transportation, future open space, parks, trails, wildlife corridors, and wildlife corridor study areas originates from the cities of Buckeye and Surprise.  These plans have been formally adopted and presented in the City of Buckeye General Plan 2040 [1]; the City of Buckeye transportation master plan [2]; the City of Buckeye Parks and Recreation Master Plan 2016 [3]; the City of Surprise General Plan 2035 [4]; and the City of Surprise Parks and Recreation Master Plan 2015 [5].  

Spatial data used to illustrate floodways, floodplains, and future regional plans for flood hazard mitigation (known as Area Drainage Master Plans) originates from the Flood Control District of Maricopa County.  Citations are provided within the StoryMap collection.

Spatial data used to illustrate existing and planned developments within municipal boundaries, and on unincorporated lands in Maricopa County, originates from the Maricopa Association of Governments' open data portal at: https://geodata-azmag.opendata.arcgis.com/.

sourceCandidates.description:

Preserving connectivity—for both wildlife and people—is essential to countering the ecological impacts of fragmentation and isolation. By working together on thoughtful connectivity solutions, we can shape a future that supports community well-being, economic vitality, and long-term conservation of the region’s rich natural heritage.  

This conservation and urban growth story is told through a collection of topics, which collectively provide an in-depth introduction to the White Tank Mountains Conservancy's CI. 

A Connectivity Vision - In the first story of our collection, you will be introduced to a collaborative vision for connectivity—a Conceptual Wildlife Linkage—aimed at preserving connections between the White Tank Mountains ecosystem and the surrounding Sonoran Desert in a rapidly expanding urban region of western Maricopa County, in Arizona, USA. The opener for our StoryMap collection outlines a compelling vision for ecologically sustainable desert cities that intentionally integrate natural and built environments to leverage ecosystem services supporting community well-being, economic vitality, and long-term conservation of the region's rich natural heritage.

Solutions Through Collaboration -  This story introduces WTMC's guiding principles—Conserve, Connect, Collaborate—along with our core values and a range of strategies we employ to engage diverse interests across the region surrounding the White Tank Mountains. Our mission is to inspire all to conserve and enjoy the natural and cultural resources of the White Tank Mountains. WTMC strives to connect people with the land, and in this story, you will discover how to engage with our CI and help us turn vision into action.

What's At Stake - a summary of the unique biodiversity, recreation values, and ecosystem services that the Connectivity Initiative seeks to protect in the White Tank Mountains region. 

Booming Cities - a detailed look at planned urban growth within the Connectivity Initiative's focal area and the reason the connectivity imperative exists.

Spatial data used in maps to illustrate future land use, future transportation, future open space, parks, trails, wildlife corridors, and wildlife corridor study areas originates from the cities of Buckeye and Surprise.  These plans have been formally adopted and presented in the City of Buckeye General Plan 2040 [1]; the City of Buckeye transportation master plan [2]; the City of Buckeye Parks and Recreation Master Plan 2016 [3]; the City of Surprise General Plan 2035 [4]; and the City of Surprise Parks and Recreation Master Plan 2015 [5].

The WTMC employed a collaborative planning effort to develop the Conceptual Wildlife Linkage presented in A Connectivity Vision.  The Arizona Game and Fish Department (AZGFD) provided technical expertise on wildlife and linkage design, and best practices. Local landowners, developers, municipalities, and the Arizona State Land Department collaborated on how to align land use plans with the biological recommendations for the linkage. WTMC developed the spatial datasets for the linkage concept. This work is presented in the Conceptual Wildlife Linkage Report [6].  Additional information on Arizona's wildlife corridors or "linkages" is available from the Arizona Game and Fish Department at: https://www.azgfd.com/wildlife/planning/habitatconnectivity/.

sourceCandidates.title: TNRIS Texas NAIP Aerial Imagery 2018 (0.6 Meter)
sourceCandidates.description: This orthoimagery dataset was acquired by the National Agriculture Imagery Program (NAIP) during the 2018 agricultural growing season and through the winter and spring of 2019 (May 2018 - April 2019), resulting in a mix of leaf-on/leaf-off conditions statewide in Texas. The entire dataset is available at 60-cm (2-foot) pixel resolution. This pixel resolution allows the user to identify features on the ground such as sheds, helipad markings, driveways, car ports, medians, and bike lanes. The entire dataset is available as a 4-band (RGBIR) product which allows the user to display the imagery in natural color (RGB) or color infrared (IRRG). Flight date is in each filename as YYYYMMDD, and each image tile covers one-4th of a USGS Digital Orthophoto Quad (DOQ), resulting in a DOQQ or approximately 16 square miles with minimum 300-meter buffers. All individual tile images are rectified in the UTM coordinate system, NAD 83, and cast into a single predetermined UTM zone. NAIP is administered by the USDA's Farm Service Agency (FSA) through the Aerial Photography Field Office (APFO) in Salt Lake City, Utah. The program was established to support FSA's strategic goals centered on agricultural production. The orthoimagery are used as a base layer for GIS programs in FSA's County Service Centers including maintenance of the Common Land Unit (CLU) boundaries. However, there are many other potential uses and applications of NAIP data as a statewide base layer of imagery in Texas. NAIP imagery are used for a wide variety of purposes throughout the state including land use planning, natural resource assessment, energy production, transportation, emergency management, ecology, air quality monitoring, etc. NAIP acquisitions in Texas are contracted based on available funding and the FSA imagery acquisition cycle. Since 2008, NAIP statewide imagery have been acquired for Texas on a 2-year cycle. This refresh rate allows the user to conduct change detection studies that can be used to monitor climate patterns such as drought or to show the rate of manmade growth on the fringes of urban development. Notes: 1) Per FSA, full coverage along the TX/MX border is not available in some areas and some DOQQ tiles contain black no data areas along the TX/MX border. Cities such as Brownsville, Pharr, south McAllen, Mission, Rio Grande City, and Eagle Pass are NOT available. 2) A fifth band of null values was created during the conversion from the uncompressed GeoTIFFs to the compressed lossless JPEG2000s. The conversion was performed in ERDAS Imagine which does not exhibit the fifth band. The fifth band is however revealed in ArcGIS and should be ignored. 3) The 2018-19 NAIP dataset does NOT include compressed county mosaics (CCMs) as they were not funded by the program this time. CCMs may become available at a later date.

sourceCandidates.title: Structures in Floodplain Dashboard
sourceCandidates.description:
This dashboard provides a comprehensive summary of structures located within the floodplain in Bexar County, TX. It visually represents and organizes data on buildings and properties that are situated in areas prone to flooding. The dashboard aims to offer valuable insights for planning, risk assessment, and emergency management by highlighting the specific locations and characteristics of these structures.

The San Antonio Office of Emergency Management conducted a study to determine how many structures are within or intersect the FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area (100 year floodplain) in San Antonio and Bexar County, TX. Locations of structures in floodplain were identified using FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area (flood zones A, AE, AO, AH) (area 35,104 acres), and Texas Google Imagery provided by the Texas Natural Resources Information System (TNRIS). Some of the structures such as miscellaneous residential improvements (e.g., detached open porches, detached garages, detached carports and sheds), electrical transformers, canopies, gas station canopies, gazebos, and small trailers were excluded from the study results. Structures in floodplain were further intersected with Bexar County Appraisal District parcel data and categorized by parcel land use type.

sourceCandidates.title: Airbnb's contribution to an affordable housing crisis in tx
sourceCandidates.description: Airbnb has contributed to an affordable housing crisis within Austin, Texas, since it was launched in 2008. After its launch, Airbnb has acquired at least 11 lawsuits, with the majority of cases taking place within the last two years. Airbnb has a huge effect on affordable housing, and it is similar to gentrification in how it slowly increases the value of an area. This makes housing unaffordable when the prices continually rise, and it becomes out of price range even more than it already has for many people and even current residents. “Indigenous residents” are pushed out due to financial restrictions due to this inherent property rise. A Harvard Business Review all across the U.S found that Airbnb is having a detrimental impact on housing stock because it encourages landlords to move their properties out of the “long-term rental and for-sale markets” and into the “short-term rental market.” A separate U.S. study found that a 1% increase in Airbnb listings leads to a 0.018% increase in rents and a 0.026% increase in housing prices. On the outside, it might not look like a lot, but it eventually keeps increasing to an unaffordable amount, especially for those looking to rent long-term or buy. (Barker, Gary. “The Airbnb Effect on Housing and Rent.” Forbes, Forbes Magazine, 20 Feb. 2024.)

sourceCandidates.title: Tale of Two Sides - Austin
sourceCandidates.description: Context: Many of the trends we see todays maps are directly caused by historical events from the 1900's. Laws and cultural norms shaped these inequalities and force people to certain areas with less opportunities and more control. Since the 1920's, east Austin was used as an area to house primarily non-whites. Laws were made that followed the doctrine of legally segregated public services proposed in Austin's first comprehensive plan (1928). They made a district in the east to house black people because they were viewed as a problem. Hispanics were also pushed to the east, but not as explicitly. This differs from west Austin, which had a high white population. Also, many neighborhoods west of downtown created racially restrictive housing rules, written into property deeds, that prohibited the sale of homes to nonwhite residents. This early historical divide is still present and can be noticed in every map regarding wealth or demographics. The white population of Austin is still relatively high throughout the years, while the black population has gone down.
